5 min read
Just now

--

How I used AI to finally create a health record worth carrying into every medical conversation I’ll ever have

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I was diagnosed with Multiple Myeloma in May of 2023.
My life is on the line. I don’t say that for effect — I say it because it’s the reason any of what follows matters.
Since the diagnosis I’ve had chemotherapy, a stem cell transplant, monthly infusions, and I’m now preparing for CAR-T therapy in August. I take five prescription medications and somewhere between eight and twelve supplements depending on the month and what the latest conversation with my oncologist or a healthcare AI has surfaced.
I could not have told you, with confidence, exactly what all of those were. Not the dosages. Not why I started each one. Not what I’d stopped and why.
That’s a problem when your life depends on their interactions.

Ten Minutes
My oncologist is great and she’s really smart. I want to be clear about that.
She also has roughly ten minutes per appointment to spend with me.
That’s not a criticism. It’s just the structural reality of oncology care. The continuity — the memory of what I’ve tried, why I tried it, what got flagged, what I stopped taking — that lives with me, not with her. She’s managing a patient panel that would make most people’s heads spin, staying current in a field that moves faster than almost any other in medicine. I don’t expect her to track my supplement decisions.
That’s my job.
There’s also the integrative side of things, which is its own territory. I believe in standard of care. I also believe you can learn something, even something small, from almost anyone — including researchers and practitioners working outside the conventional system. I understand there are profiteers and gurus online who probably don’t know what they’re talking about. I read carefully anyway.
Navigating all of that honestly, with a serious diagnosis in play, requires keeping a very careful record of what you’re doing and why.
I had a ChatGPT project sitting idle called “My Entire Vitamin and Supplement Universe.” I’d opened it months ago and never gotten to it. Last night I finally sat down and did the work.

What Actually Happened
I didn’t want a one-time list that I would lose. I wanted something I could actually use in real life — something I could drop into any future conversation with a healthcare AI, a new specialist, or an emergency room physician who needs to know what I’m taking before they give me something that interacts badly with it.
ChatGPT’s opening move surprised me. Instead of asking me to type everything out, it told me to gather every bottle I owned, put them on a surface, and take one overhead photo. Then we’d go bottle by bottle.
It reframed the whole session immediately: we’re building Version 1.0 today, not a rough draft.
So I assembled everything on my wife’s desk. Photographed the front label of each bottle, then the supplement facts panel. Talked through each one — the dose, where I bought it, why I started it, what had changed. At some point I stopped paying close attention to its questions and just started uploading photos, dosages, and whatever thoughts came to mind, moving as fast as I could.
I figured it would compile everything into something cohesive.
Hopefully, right?
Some were simple. Others had whole stories attached — a probiotic I’d bought and never taken because RadicalHealth AI flagged it as too risky for an immune system already under strain. A medication I’d been taking seven days on, seven days off until two weeks ago, when my oncologist and I decided to stop everything and move toward CAR-T.
All of it went in. Current. Discontinued. Purchased but never started.
The categories matter because the history matters.

The Reframe That Stuck
Somewhere in the middle of the session, ChatGPT suggested we stop thinking of what we were building as a medication list and start thinking of it as a decision journal.
For every item: why I started it, what evidence I’d reviewed, who recommended it, what the oncology team said, what the healthcare AI flagged, and what I ultimately decided.
Not merged together. Separated. Facts in one place, physician recommendations in another, AI recommendations in another, my own decisions in another.
The core principle the document ended up with: this records what Paul actually does, not what anyone believes he should do.
That’s the only version that’s actually useful when someone needs to make a real medical decision based on it.
The project isn’t finished. We got through the prescription medications and most of the supplements before I ran out of steam. What I do have is a structured file I can drop into any future AI conversation and pick up exactly where I left off.
That alone was worth the evening.

You Probably Need This Too
You don’t need a Multiple Myeloma diagnosis to need this document.
You need it if you’re taking more than a handful of things and you’ve lost track of why you started any of them. You need it if you see more than one doctor who doesn’t necessarily know what the other has recommended. You need it if you’re using healthcare AI — which can do remarkable things with your health history, but only if your health history is actually organized.
The method is simple. Gather the bottles. Photograph them. Go one at a time. Record why you started each one. Record what you’ve stopped. Keep the history — don’t delete anything, just change the status.
Build something you can upload to any AI or give to any physician and have them understand your complete picture in five minutes.
The goal isn’t a perfect record on day one.
I’m going to finish mine tomorrow but now I’ve got a way to finally keep it all up to date in one place. It’s not really hyperbole but my life kind of depends on it.

I’ve been using AI as a health research tool for about two years — not as a replacement for medical care, but as a way to show up to medical care better prepared. If you want to see how I approach that, the Clear AI Starter Pack is a good place to start. Free, no strings.
